summary:
Cayonu Tepesi is a large Pre-Pottery Neolithic settlement in southeastern Anatolia, near the Tigris River headwaters in modern Turkey, with occupation spanning approximately 8500–6200 BCE. It is one of the most comprehensively excavated early Neolithic sites in the region and has yielded evidence for some of the earliest copper working in the world, with small objects of hammered native copper among the finds. The site also preserves multiple architectural phases that trace the evolution of building design across several millennia.

Cayonu is notable for a large communal building interpreted as a cult or feasting structure, as well as evidence of what may be ritual deposits of human skulls, suggesting complex mortuary and religious practices. The site's long occupation sequence documents the gradual transition from pre-agricultural to fully agricultural subsistence, making it one of the key reference points for understanding the Neolithic transition in Anatolia. Its proximity to other significant PPNB sites such as Nevali Cori underscores the cultural vitality of the upper Euphrates and Tigris corridor during this period.
